published.text:
### Hero
Heading: CRM Cleanup vs Lead Follow-Up for Real Estate Teams
Body: If your real-estate team cannot trust the CRM, more lead-follow-up automation usually makes the mess louder instead of fixing it. But if the pipeline is basically usable and the bigger leak is slow first response, waiting for a perfect cleanup can cost more than starting with lead follow-up now. That is the real decision. Not “which page sounds more strategic?” The question is which problem is already more expensive today: messy stages, routing, and ownership inside the CRM, or leads going cold because nobody is responding and nurturing quickly enough.
Sub: Below: when CRM cleanup should come first, when lead follow-up should come first, how this decision page stays distinct from the broader real-estate parent plus the already-live cleanup and lead-response guides, and what adjacent proof honestly supports the choice without pretending there is already a published real-estate-specific comparison case study.

### Core sections
- Frames the page as an exact workflow-priority decision for real-estate teams choosing between two already-live sibling fixes: CRM cleanup and lead follow-up.
- Keeps the comparison bounded to the real buyer question: whether the current loss is pipeline mess, weak routing, duplicates, and ownership confusion — or slow first response and inconsistent early nurture.
- Uses the live real-estate cluster itself as the primary proof frame, with the existing CRM cleanup page, lead-response page, and first-project page doing most of the support work, and the published e-commerce CRM case study only backing the underlying stage-truth / ownership mechanics.
- Includes fit framing, a side-by-side table, sequencing guidance when both problems are real, FAQ, and related links so the page answers a distinct decision instead of cannibalizing the cleanup or lead-response explainers.

### FAQ
Subhead: Practical questions from real-estate teams deciding whether pipeline cleanup or faster lead follow-up should come first.
Topics: the clearest sign cleanup should come first, the clearest sign lead follow-up should come first, whether a small phase-one version of both is possible, how this differs from the sibling cleanup and lead-response pages, and what a focused first project usually costs.

### Final CTA
Heading: Need help deciding whether your real-estate team should fix CRM cleanup or lead follow-up first?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will look at where your team is actually losing momentum today — inside stage/routing chaos or inside response speed and early nurture — and tell you which smaller project is most likely to pay off first.

edits.description: Shipped because broad backlog reconciliation surfaced one exact tracked buyer-decision query already living inside the real-estate CRM-cleanup prompt set: “Should a real estate team start with CRM cleanup or lead follow-up?”

Keeps a disciplined distinction from `/crm-cleanup-and-routing-for-real-estate-teams` and `/real-estate-lead-response-and-follow-up-automation` by staying one step earlier in the decision chain: which fix should come first, not how either workflow works in isolation.

Proof framing stays honest: the live real-estate cluster provides the most direct adjacency, while the WheelsFeels CRM case study only supports the general principle that messy statuses and weak ownership make automation noisier instead of better.

Tone stays practical and operational: no fake proptech claims, no invented real-estate case study, and no pressure to do both projects at once when one narrower first move is usually the smarter choice.
